messages.content:
You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following school canteen cashless payment system notice for GDPR compliance:

"The school uses a cashless payment system for canteen purchases. Pupils are assigned a unique ID linked to their canteen account. Parents top up accounts online. Purchase history is visible to parents via the parent portal. Data is retained for the current academic year plus 1 year. We use CaterApp as our canteen management provider."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PARTIAL] Contract (Art. 6(1)(b)) for payment processing and public task (Art. 6(1)(e)) for school administration are implied but not stated.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] Food purchase history can reveal dietary requirements linked to religion or health (e.g., halal, gluten-free, diabetic meals). No acknowledgement or safeguard stated.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Canteen payment processing with parental visibility is a clear and specific purpose.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Unique ID and purchase history are proportionate for cashless canteen management.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] Current academic year plus 1 year is clearly stated.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [FAIL] No data subject or parental rights mentioned.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PARTIAL] CaterApp named as provider but no DPA reference stated.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] Not addressed. CaterApp infrastructure may involve non-EEA processing.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not addressed. Schools processing pupil data are required to appoint a DPO as public authorities.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (lawful basis not stated), Art. 9 (dietary inference risk not addressed), Art. 15-21 (no rights), Art. 28 (CaterApp DPA not referenced), Art. 46 (transfer safeguards absent), Art. 37 (school DPO required), Art. 33 (no breach notification)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- State lawful basis for each processing activity
- Acknowledge dietary data may reveal religious or health information and state safeguards
- Add parental rights section including access, rectification, and erasure
- Reference CaterApp DPA and confirm EU/UK data processing
- Address international transfer safeguards if CaterApp processes outside UK/EEA
- Disclose school DPO contact
- Add breach notification commitment

You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following immigration law firm client data notice for GDPR compliance:

"VisaLegal processes your name, passport, nationality, immigration history, and family details to provide immigration legal advice and submit visa applications. We process this under our contract with you and legal obligations as a regulated law firm. As immigration data reveals nationality and may relate to political opinions or family status, we handle all data with enhanced care. Files are retained for 6 years after matter closure. You have the right to access, correct, restrict, port, and erase your data, and to object to processing, by contacting privacy@visalegal.com. Our DPO is at dpo@visalegal.com. We use a case management system (Clio, EU-hosted) under a DPA. We notify the ICO within 72 hours of a breach."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PASS] Contract performance (Art. 6(1)(b)) and legal obligation (Art. 6(1)(c)) both stated and appropriate for regulated immigration legal services.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PASS] Nationality, political opinions, and family status are correctly identified as potentially special category. "Enhanced care" is stated — though the specific Art. 9(2) basis and concrete safeguards should be more precisely defined.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Immigration advice and visa application submission are clear and specific purposes.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Data collected is standard and proportionate for immigration legal services.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] 6-year post-matter retention stated and grounded in professional legal regulatory requirements.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PASS] Access (Art. 15), rectification (Art. 16), erasure (Art. 17), restriction (Art. 18), portability (Art. 20), and objection (Art. 21) all listed.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] Clio named as case management system provider with EU hosting and DPA.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] EU-hosted Clio confirms EEA processing.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [PASS] DPO contact at dpo@visalegal.com disclosed.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [PASS] 72-hour ICO notification stated.

VERDICT: Partially Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 9 (Art. 9(2) basis and concrete safeguards for special category data not precisely stated)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Specify the Art. 9(2) basis for processing nationality and political opinion data (likely Art. 9(2)(g) substantial public interest or Art. 9(2)(b) employment/legal basis under national law)
- Define concrete safeguards beyond "enhanced care" — e.g., access controls, encryption standards, staff training

You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following wearable health device privacy policy for GDPR compliance:

"VitalBand collects your heart rate, blood oxygen, sleep data, and activity levels continuously. We store 90 days of detailed data on our servers. Aggregated and anonymised data may be used for health research. We share your data with healthcare providers if you choose to integrate with your medical record system. Our servers are in Japan."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [FAIL] No lawful basis stated for any processing activity.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [FAIL] Heart rate, blood oxygen, and sleep data constitute health data — special category data under Art. 9. No explicit consent or Art. 9(2) basis is stated.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Personal health monitoring, research, and healthcare provider integration are stated. The research purpose requires separate consent and is not sufficiently described.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Continuous collection of detailed biometric data for 90 days requires proportionality justification — not provided.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] 90-day detailed data retention stated. Retention of aggregated or research data is not addressed.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [FAIL] No data subject rights stated.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PARTIAL] Healthcare providers named as optional integration recipients. Research partners and data processing relationships not described.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] Servers in Japan disclosed but no transfer safeguard mechanism stated. Japan has an EU adequacy decision — this should be cited.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not addressed. Large-scale processing of continuous biometric health data almost certainly requires a DPO.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (no lawful basis), Art. 9 (health and biometric data without valid Art. 9(2) basis), Art. 5 (continuous collection proportionality unjustified, research data retention absent), Art. 15-21 (rights absent), Art. 28 (research partner relationships undefined), Art. 46 (Japan transfer safeguard not cited), Art. 37 (DPO required), Art. 33 (no breach notification)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Obtain and document explicit consent for continuous health data collection under Art. 9(2)(a)
- State lawful basis for each processing activity
- Justify continuous data collection or provide user controls to limit scope
- Add retention period for aggregated and research data
- Add full data subject rights section
- Describe research partner relationships and confirm anonymisation standard
- Cite Japan adequacy decision as transfer basis
- Appoint and disclose DPO
- Add breach notification commitment

You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following not-for-profit food bank data collection notice for GDPR compliance:

"FoodFirst collects the name, household composition, and dietary requirements of individuals and families accessing our services. We collect this information to provide appropriate food parcels and plan our service capacity. Data is not shared with any third parties. Dietary requirements including allergies and religious restrictions are collected with your verbal consent. Records are kept for 12 months and then deleted."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PARTIAL] Legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f)) or vital interests (Art. 6(1)(d)) implied for service delivery to vulnerable individuals. Neither is explicitly stated.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] Dietary requirements including religious restrictions constitute data revealing religious beliefs — special category data under Art. 9(1). Verbal consent is described but is not documented or verifiable, and the Art. 9(2) basis is not explicitly stated.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Food parcel provision and service capacity planning are clear and specific purposes.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Name, household composition, and dietary requirements are proportionate to the service delivery purpose.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] 12-month retention with deletion is clearly stated.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [FAIL] No data subject rights mentioned.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] "Not shared with any third parties" is clearly stated.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] Not applicable given no third-party sharing.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not addressed. Processing of vulnerable individuals' data at scale may require a DPO.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (lawful basis not stated), Art. 9 (religious dietary data requires documented Art. 9(2) basis — verbal consent is insufficient without records), Art. 15-21 (rights absent), Art. 37 (DPO not addressed), Art. 33 (no breach notification)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- State explicit lawful basis — likely vital interests (Art. 6(1)(d)) for vulnerable individuals in need or legitimate interest
- Replace verbal consent with documented written or digital consent for religious/dietary special category data
- Add a simple data subject rights section appropriate to service users
- Confirm DPO requirement and disclose if applicable
- Add breach notification commitment

You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following private members club privacy notice for GDPR compliance:

"The Club collects your name, address, date of birth, occupation, proposer details, and payment information as part of the membership application process. Successful applicants' details are circulated to the membership committee for approval. Member records are held for the duration of membership plus 5 years. We do not share your data with external parties. You may update your details by contacting the membership secretary."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PARTIAL] Contract (Art. 6(1)(b)) implied for membership management. Legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f)) implied for circulation to the membership committee. Neither is explicitly stated.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] Occupation and proposer details may reveal political, religious, or professional affiliations. No acknowledgement or safeguard stated.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Membership application, committee approval, and member record management are stated as purposes.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Occupation and proposer details require justification as they go beyond standard membership administration data.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] Membership duration plus 5 years is clearly stated.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Only update of details (rectification) referenced. Other rights absent.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] "Do not share with external parties" is clearly stated. Internal committee circulation is disclosed.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] Not applicable given no external sharing.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not addressed.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (lawful basis not stated), Art. 9 (occupation/affiliation inference risk not addressed), Art. 5 (occupation and proposer data necessity not justified), Art. 15-21 (most rights absent), Art. 37 (DPO not addressed), Art. 33 (no breach notification)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- State explicit lawful basis for each processing activity
- Justify collection of occupation and proposer details and acknowledge potential affiliation inference
- Add a full data subject rights section
- Confirm DPO requirement and disclose if applicable
- Add breach notification commitment

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following pension advisory firm client privacy notice for GDPR compliance:

"RetireWell collects your name, national insurance number, employment history, pension scheme details, and investment preferences to provide retirement planning advice. We process this under our FCA-regulated contract with you. All financial advice records are retained for 6 years per FCA rules. You have the right to access, correct, restrict, port, and object to your data. Contact compliance@retirewell.com. Our DPO is at dpo@retirewell.com. We use Salesforce Financial Services Cloud (UK instance) under a signed DPA. We notify the FCA and ICO within 72 hours of a breach."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PASS] Contract performance (Art. 6(1)(b)) for advisory services and legal obligation (Art. 6(1)(c)) for FCA regulatory requirements both stated.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] Pension and employment history data can reveal disability, ill-health retirement, or financial vulnerability. No acknowledgement or safeguard stated.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Retirement planning advice and FCA regulatory compliance are clearly stated purposes.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Data collected is standard and proportionate for retirement planning advisory services.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] 6-year FCA-mandated retention is clearly stated.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Access (Art. 15), rectification (Art. 16), restriction (Art. 18), portability (Art. 20), and objection (Art. 21) listed. Erasure (Art. 17) absent — likely limited by FCA retention obligations, but this should be explained.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] Salesforce Financial Services Cloud UK instance named under a signed DPA.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] UK instance confirmed. Post-Brexit UK adequacy for EU clients should be referenced if applicable.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [PASS] DPO contact at dpo@retirewell.com disclosed.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [PASS] 72-hour notification to FCA and ICO both stated.

VERDICT: Partially Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 9 (pension and employment data sensitivity not addressed), Art. 15-21 (erasure right absent without explanation)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Acknowledge that pension and employment history may reveal disability or ill-health data and state appropriate safeguards
- Add the erasure right with a clear explanation that it is limited by FCA record-keeping obligations under Art. 17(3)(b)

You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following photography studio client privacy notice for GDPR compliance:

"SnapStudio collects your name, contact details, and session booking information to manage your photography appointments. We retain photographs taken during sessions for 2 years unless you request earlier deletion. With your explicit consent, we may use images in our portfolio or marketing materials. Payment is processed via Square (EU). Contact hello@snapstudio.com with any data queries."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PARTIAL] Contract (Art. 6(1)(b)) implied for booking management. Consent (Art. 6(1)(a)) stated for portfolio/marketing use of images. Neither is explicitly named for general data processing.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] Photographs of individuals can constitute biometric data under Art. 9 if processed for the purpose of unique identification. If images are used in facial recognition or AI analysis, this requires explicit Art. 9 consent. The portfolio use of images should also consider whether subjects include minors.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Booking management, image retention, and optional portfolio/marketing use are clearly separated purposes.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Name, contact details, booking information, and session photographs are proportionate to a photography studio service.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] 2-year image retention with earlier deletion on request is clearly stated.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Only a contact email is provided. Specific rights (access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection) are not individually listed. Right to withdraw consent for marketing use should be prominently stated.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PARTIAL] Square named as payment processor. No DPA reference provided.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] Square processes data in the US — no transfer safeguard mechanism stated.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not addressed. Likely not required at studio scale, but this should be confirmed.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Partially Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 9 (biometric risk of photographs not addressed, minor image consideration absent), Art. 15-21 (rights not individually listed, consent withdrawal not highlighted), Art. 28 (Square DPA not referenced), Art. 46 (Square US transfer safeguards absent), Art. 37 (DPO assessment not stated), Art. 33 (no breach notification)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Confirm that photographs are not processed for biometric identification; if so, obtain explicit Art. 9 consent
- Add policy for photographing minors including parental consent requirements
- Add full data subject rights section prominently including how to withdraw portfolio/marketing consent
- Reference Square DPA and confirm EU data processing or address US transfer safeguards (SCCs)
- Confirm DPO assessment outcome
- Add breach notification commitment

Assess the following gig economy delivery platform privacy policy for GDPR compliance:

"SwiftDeliver collects name, bank account details, national ID, vehicle registration, and real-time GPS location from delivery partners. We use this to manage deliveries, process payments, and comply with our legal obligations as an employer. GPS tracking is active only during active delivery sessions. Earnings records are retained for 6 years for tax compliance. You have the right to access, correct, erase, port, restrict, and object to your data. Contact gdpr@swiftdeliver.com or our DPO at dpo@swiftdeliver.com. We use AWS EU (Ireland). We notify the ICO within 72 hours of a breach. Our sub-processors are listed at swiftdeliver.com/processors."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PASS] Contract performance (Art. 6(1)(b)) for delivery management and payments. Legal obligation (Art. 6(1)(c)) for tax compliance. Multiple bases correctly applied.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] National ID can imply ethnic origin or nationality. No acknowledgement or safeguard stated.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Delivery management, payment processing, and tax compliance are clearly separated purposes.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] GPS tracking limited to active delivery sessions is a strong minimisation measure.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] 6-year earnings record retention stated and grounded in tax legal obligations.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PASS] Access (Art. 15), rectification (Art. 16), erasure (Art. 17), portability (Art. 20), restriction (Art. 18), and objection (Art. 21) all listed.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] Sub-processors listed at a dedicated URL.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] AWS EU Ireland confirms EEA processing.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [PASS] DPO contact at dpo@swiftdeliver.com disclosed.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [PASS] 72-hour ICO notification stated.

VERDICT: Partially Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 9 (national ID inference risk not addressed)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Acknowledge that national ID data may imply ethnic origin or nationality and state appropriate safeguards or processing restrictions

Assess the following dental practice patient privacy notice for GDPR compliance:

"Bright Smiles Dental collects your name, date of birth, contact details, medical history, and dental records to provide dental treatment. We process health data as necessary for the provision of healthcare under Art. 9(2)(h). Patient records are retained for 10 years after the last appointment as required by dental professional regulations. You have the right to access, correct, restrict, port, and erase your data, and to object to processing, by contacting privacy@brightsmiles.com. Our DPO is at dpo@brightsmiles.com. We use Dentally practice management software (UK-hosted) under a signed DPA. We notify the ICO within 72 hours of a breach."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PASS] Healthcare necessity (Art. 6(1)(c)) via dental professional regulations implied. Contract (Art. 6(1)(b)) also applicable for private dental treatment.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PASS] Health and dental records correctly identified as special category data. Art. 9(2)(h) (healthcare provision) explicitly cited as the processing basis.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Dental treatment provision is a clear and specific purpose.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Name, DOB, contact details, medical history, and dental records are proportionate and necessary for dental care.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] 10-year post-last-appointment retention stated and grounded in dental professional regulatory requirements.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PASS] Access (Art. 15), rectification (Art. 16), erasure (Art. 17), restriction (Art. 18), portability (Art. 20), and objection (Art. 21) all listed.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] Dentally named as practice management software provider with UK hosting and signed DPA.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] UK-hosted Dentally confirms no EEA transfer concerns.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [PASS] DPO contact at dpo@brightsmiles.com disclosed.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [PASS] 72-hour ICO notification stated.

VERDICT: Compliant
VIOLATIONS: None
RECOMMENDATIONS: No changes required. Consider explicitly stating Art. 6(1)(b) or (c) as the lawful basis alongside the Art. 9(2)(h) basis for additional transparency, and adding the right to lodge a complaint with the ICO (Art. 77).

Assess the following logistics and freight company privacy policy for GDPR compliance:

"FreightCo collects shipper and recipient names, addresses, and contact details to arrange and track shipments. We also collect driver names and GPS location data for fleet management. Customer data is processed under our contract with you. Driver data is processed under the employment contract and legitimate interest for fleet safety. Shipment records are retained for 7 years for tax and legal purposes. Driver location data is retained for 30 days. You have the right to access, correct, and erase your data. Contact dataprotection@freightco.com. Our DPO is at dpo@freightco.com. We use SAP TM (EU-hosted) under a DPA. We notify the ICO within 72 hours of a breach."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PASS] Contract performance (Art. 6(1)(b)) for customer data. Employment contract (Art. 6(1)(b)) and legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f)) for driver data. Multiple bases correctly applied to distinct data subject categories.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] Driver GPS data collected continuously could reveal religious observance, health-related patterns, or union activity. No acknowledgement or safeguard stated.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Shipment arrangement, tracking, and fleet management/safety are clearly stated as distinct purposes.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Data collected is proportionate to freight logistics and fleet management purposes.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] Shipment records: 7 years (tax/legal). Driver location: 30 days. Both clearly stated and justified.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Access (Art. 15), rectification (Art. 16), and erasure (Art. 17) listed. Rights to portability (Art. 20), restriction (Art. 18), and objection (Art. 21) absent. Objection is particularly relevant for driver legitimate interest processing.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] SAP TM named as logistics management processor with EU hosting and DPA.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] EU-hosted SAP TM confirms EEA processing.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [PASS] DPO contact at dpo@freightco.com disclosed.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [PASS] 72-hour ICO notification stated.

VERDICT: Partially Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 9 (driver location data inference risk not addressed), Art. 15-21 (portability, restriction, objection rights absent)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Acknowledge that driver GPS patterns may reveal sensitive information and state appropriate safeguards
- Add rights to portability, restriction, and objection — and ensure the right to object is prominently stated for driver legitimate interest processing
